items.q: What is the dramatic function of a soliloquy?
items.correct: It reveals a character's inner thoughts directly to the audience while the character is alone on stage — a convention granting access to private psychology.
items.misconception: A soliloquy is any long speech a character gives.
items.random_wrong: A soliloquy is when characters argue with each other.
items.why: Students confuse soliloquy (alone, inner thoughts) with monologue (long speech, possibly to others). The key feature is solitude and direct access to thought — losing this loses the convention's force.

items.correct: When the audience knows something a character does not, creating tension between the character's words/actions and the audience's understanding.
items.misconception: Dramatic irony is when something ironic happens in a play.
items.random_wrong: Dramatic irony is when characters speak in dramatic voices.
items.why: Students treat 'dramatic irony' as just irony in a drama. The defining feature is the gap between audience and character knowledge — without that gap, it isn't dramatic irony.

items.correct: Their account is undermined within the text by ignorance, bias, self-deception or deceit, so the reader must read against what they say.
items.misconception: An unreliable narrator is one who tells lies in the story.
items.random_wrong: An unreliable narrator is when the author makes mistakes.
items.why: Unreliability is structural — the text gives signals the narrator is wrong (e.g. internal contradictions). Lying intentionally is one kind, but blind spots and self-deception count too.

items.q: Which Romantic critic coined the phrase 'motiveless malignity' for Iago?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Samuel Taylor Coleridge. “William Hazlitt” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: How many soliloquies does Iago have in the play?
items.why: It's Eight. “Four” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Approximately how many times is the word 'honest' applied to Iago in the play?
items.why: The real figure is 50. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
